exercises.description: Seated plate-loaded lever pullover that moves the arms in an overhead-to-hip arc to lengthen and contract the lats while also engaging the pectorals and serratus anterior. Performed on a lever machine with the torso supported by a backrest for stability.

exercises.name: Lever Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Set the machine: choose an appropriate weight, adjust the seat height so the pivot of the lever lines up with your shoulder joint and the handles start near your hips. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Set the machine: choose an appropriate weight, adjust the seat height so the pivot of the lever lines up with your shoulder joint and the handles start near your hips.
Sit upright with your back pressed into the seat pad, feet flat on the floor, and chest up; grasp the handles with a neutral or slightly pronated grip.
Start with your elbows slightly bent and arms resting at your sides (not locked), maintaining a neutral wrist and steady grip.
Exhale and lift the handles out to your sides in a controlled arc, leading with the elbow so the upper arm reaches about parallel to the floor (shoulder height).
Keep your torso still and avoid shrugging your shoulders; use your delts to move the weight rather than momentum or back extension.
Pause briefly at the top with the elbows slightly higher than the wrists, feeling tension in the delts and traps for scapular stability.
Inhale and slowly lower the handles back to the start position with control, maintaining the slight elbow bend.
Repeat for the desired number of reps. Prioritize controlled tempo, full range without swinging, and maintain scapular stability throughout.
exercises.description: Seated lever lateral raise is a machine exercise that isolates the lateral head of the delts by abducting the arms out to the sides from a seated, supported position. It emphasizes controlled movement to raise the arms to about shoulder height while minimizing trunk movement.

exercises.name: Lever Alternating Pullover (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat height so the back pad supports your upper back and the lever handles sit just above/behind your head when seated upright. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Adjust the seat height so the back pad supports your upper back and the lever handles sit just above/behind your head when seated upright.
Load an appropriate plate weight and sit with your back flat against the pad, feet flat on the floor and knees bent; brace your core to maintain a neutral spine.
Grasp the right and left lever handles (one in each hand) with a neutral or overhand grip. Begin with both arms extended overhead with a slight bend in the elbows.

For each rep, exhale and pull one lever down and in a smooth arc toward your chest/upper stomach using the shoulder joint — lead with the upper arm, not the wrist, and keep the elbow slightly bent and fixed.
At the bottom of the movement, pause briefly and focus on squeezing the pectorals and lats on the working side while keeping your torso stationary.
Inhale and slowly return the lever under control back to the start (overhead) to complete the eccentric phase; avoid letting the shoulder snap back.
Alternate sides each repetition (right arm then left arm) while maintaining the same tempo, core bracing, and no excessive torso rotation or lumbar arch.
Keep movements controlled, avoid shrugging the shoulders, and reduce weight if you cannot maintain a fixed elbow angle and stable torso throughout the set.
exercises.description: Seated plate-loaded lever pullover is a single-arm/alternating machine pullover that moves the upper arm in an arc from overhead down toward the chest, emphasizing the pectorals while also recruiting the lats and serratus anterior. The movement is performed with a slight elbow bend and controlled eccentric return.

exercises.name: Lever Standing Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Adjust the machine weight so you can perform controlled reps without shoulder shrugging. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Adjust the machine weight so you can perform controlled reps without shoulder shrugging.
Stand upright beside the lever with feet hip- to shoulder-width apart, knees soft and core braced; grasp the handle with the working hand (thumb wrapped) at your side.
Keep a slight bend in the elbow and the wrist neutral; maintain an upright torso with shoulders down and back (no leaning or twisting).
Exhale and lift the arm straight out to the side in a smooth arc until the upper arm reaches roughly shoulder height (parallel to the floor).
Pause briefly at the top while keeping the scapula stable and avoiding excessive shoulder elevation.
Inhale and slowly lower the lever back to the starting position under control, resisting any momentum.
Repeat for the desired reps, then switch sides if performing single-arm sets.
exercises.description: A standing lateral raise performed on a lever/cable station that isolates the lateral head of the deltoid by lifting the arm out to the side from the body. The movement uses a controlled upward arc to shoulder height and a slow descent to emphasize the delts.

exercises.name: Lever Pronated Grip Seated Scapula Retraction Shrug (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat height and load the machine so you can reach the handles with your arms extended and feet flat on the footrests; sit upright with a neutral spine and knees bent. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Adjust the seat height and load the machine so you can reach the handles with your arms extended and feet flat on the footrests; sit upright with a neutral spine and knees bent.
Grasp the handles with a pronated (overhand) grip and let your shoulders relax down and slightly forward to set the start position.
Initiate the movement by pulling the handles back using your shoulder blades: actively retract (pinch) the scapulae together while keeping the torso stable and chest neutral.
As you retract the scapulae, elevate your shoulders into a controlled shrug (shoulder elevation) and squeeze your traps and upper back at the top for 1–2 seconds.
Slowly reverse the motion: lower the shoulders and allow the scapulae to protract back to the start position under control — do not let the weight slam forward.
Keep the arms relatively straight with a slight bend at the elbows, avoid using momentum or torso swing, and breathe out during the contraction and in during the return.
exercises.description: Seated, pronated-grip plate-loaded exercise that emphasizes scapular retraction and an upward shrug to target the trapezius and upper back. Performed from a seated position using the machine handles to actively pinch the shoulder blades together and elevate the shoulders.

tips: Every working set should reach true muscular failure.
Use slow, controlled tempo throughout the movement.
Allow full recovery between exercises - this is not cardio.

exercises.name: Barbell Squat
exercises.notes: Set the bar in a squat rack at about mid-chest height. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Set the bar in a squat rack at about mid-chest height. Position yourself under the bar so it rests across your upper traps (high-bar) or rear delts (low-bar) depending on preference.
Grip the bar with hands just outside shoulder width, elbows pointing down and chest up. Stand with feet roughly shoulder-width apart and toes pointed slightly outward.
Brace your core and take a full breath in, then unrack the bar by extending your legs and taking 1–2 controlled steps backward to clear the rack; establish a stable, shoulder-width stance.
Keep a neutral spine and an upright chest. Initiate the movement by pushing your hips back slightly and bending at the knees, sending your knees outward to track over your toes.
Descend in a controlled manner until your thighs are at least parallel to the floor (or deeper if mobility allows) while keeping weight on your midfoot/heels and maintaining a braced core.
Avoid knee collapse (valgus) — consciously push knees outward and keep toes engaged. Do not let the lower back round; maintain a neutral spine throughout.
Drive through your heels and midfoot to stand back up, exhaling as you ascend and extending hips and knees simultaneously until fully upright.
Reset your breathing and bracing between reps as needed. When finished, step forward under control and re-rack the bar securely.
Use appropriate weight, a spotter or safety pins in the rack if lifting near maximum loads, and adjust stance or bar position to reduce discomfort in shoulders or lower back.
exercises.description: Barbell back squat is a compound, standing lower-body exercise that primarily develops the quadriceps while also engaging the glutes, hamstrings and core for stability. The barbell rests across the upper back while you descend by bending hips and knees, then drive back up to standing.

exercises.name: Barbell Romanian Deadlift
exercises.notes: Stand upright holding a barbell with an overhand (or mixed) grip, feet about hip-width apart and the bar resting against your thighs; maintain a neutral spine and engaged core. Stop 1 rep short of failure (RPE 9). Spinal safety takes priority.
instructions: Stand upright holding a barbell with an overhand (or mixed) grip, feet about hip-width apart and the bar resting against your thighs; maintain a neutral spine and engaged core.
Set a small, consistent bend in the knees (soft knees) — do not bend into a squat; this is a hinge at the hips, not a knee-dominant lift.
Take a breath in, brace your core, then push your hips back while keeping the chest up and spine neutral; allow the bar to travel down close to the front of your legs.
Lower the bar until you feel a strong but safe stretch in the hamstrings (typically to mid‑shin), keeping the bar close to the body and shoulders pulled back — avoid rounding the lower back.
Pause briefly at the bottom while maintaining tension in the hamstrings and back; keep your head in a neutral alignment (looking a few feet in front of you).
Exhale and drive your hips forward by contracting the glutes and hamstrings to return to the standing start position; do not hyperextend the lower back at the top.
Control the descent and ascent on every rep, using a weight that lets you maintain a neutral spine and full hip hinge pattern.
If grip is limiting, switch to a mixed grip or use straps; always prioritize form over load and stop if you feel lumbar rounding or sharp pain.
exercises.description: A bilateral hip-hinge performed from standing that emphasizes the hamstrings by lowering a loaded barbell along the thighs with a neutral spine and slight knee bend; returned to standing by driving the hips forward and squeezing the glutes. Builds posterior chain strength and improves hip extension.

exercises.name: Lever Standing Rear Kick
exercises.notes: Stand facing the machine with the working leg next to the lever pad and feet hip-width apart; place the non-working foot on the platform for stability. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Stand facing the machine with the working leg next to the lever pad and feet hip-width apart; place the non-working foot on the platform for stability.
Grasp the machine handles or frame with both hands to stabilize the upper body and brace your core; lean forward slightly at the hips while keeping the spine neutral.
Position the lever pad against the posterior thigh of the working leg (just above the knee) with the knee slightly bent.
Exhale and drive the heel of the working leg straight back by extending at the hip, squeezing the glute at the top of the motion; avoid excessive lumbar arching or rotating the hips.
Hold the fully extended position for a brief second, then inhale and slowly return the lever to the starting position under control without letting the hips twist.
Perform the desired number of repetitions, then switch legs. Maintain a steady tempo and keep the torso stable throughout the set.
exercises.description: Standing hip-extension movement performed on a rear-kick/lever machine where one leg pushes backward against a padded lever to drive hip extension and isolate the glute. The opposite leg remains planted for balance while the core stabilizes the torso.

tips: Every working set should reach true muscular failure.
Use slow, controlled tempo throughout the movement.
Allow full recovery between exercises - this is not cardio.

exercises.name: Chin-ups (narrow parallel grip)
exercises.notes: Start by grasping parallel handles overhead with a narrow neutral grip (palms facing each other) and arms fully extended while hanging from the bar; cross your ankles or keep feet together and maintain a slight hollow body with ribs down.
instructions: Start by grasping parallel handles overhead with a narrow neutral grip (palms facing each other) and arms fully extended while hanging from the bar; cross your ankles or keep feet together and maintain a slight hollow body with ribs down.
Brace your core and retract your shoulder blades slightly to remove slack before initiating the pull (scapular set).
Exhale and pull your elbows down and back, driving the elbows toward your hips while focusing on pulling with the lats rather than just the arms.
Continue the pull until your chin clears or reaches the level of the handles and your chest approaches the grips; pause and squeeze the shoulder blades together at the top for 1 second.
Inhale and lower yourself slowly and under control back to a full hang with arms fully extended, maintaining shoulder tension and avoiding a sudden drop.
Keep the torso stable and avoid excessive swinging or kipping; if needed, use a controlled bend in the knees or a slight leg cross to prevent momentum.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, focusing on smooth tempo, full range of motion, and consistent breathing.
exercises.description: A narrow, neutral-grip chin-up performed from a vertical hang that emphasizes the latissimus dorsi while also engaging the arms and upper back. Pull the body up until the chin clears the handles, then lower under control to a full hang.

exercises.name: Decline Push-Up
exercises.notes: Extend your body into a straight line from head to heels, engaging your core and glutes to maintain a rigid plank position throughout the movement.
instructions: Place your feet on a bench or elevated surface and position your hands on the floor slightly wider than shoulder-width apart, fingers pointing forward.
Extend your body into a straight line from head to heels, engaging your core and glutes to maintain a rigid plank position throughout the movement.
Lower your chest toward the floor by bending your elbows at a 30-45 degree angle from your torso, keeping your head neutral and spine aligned.
Press through your palms to push back up to the starting position, fully extending your arms without locking the elbows.
Maintain core tension throughout and avoid letting your hips sag or pike up during the movement.
exercises.description: A decline push-up performed with feet elevated on a bench, placing greater emphasis on the upper chest and anterior deltoids. The elevated foot position increases the difficulty compared to standard push-ups while targeting the pectorals, with secondary engagement from the triceps, anterior delts, serratus anterior, and core for stabilization.

exercises.name: Lying Reverse Leg Extension
exercises.notes: Lie supine (face up) on a flat surface with legs extended, arms by your sides for stability, and head resting comfortably.
instructions: Lie supine (face up) on a flat surface with legs extended, arms by your sides for stability, and head resting comfortably.
Brace your core by engaging the abs to stabilize the pelvis and maintain a neutral lower back throughout the movement.
Keeping legs straight or with a slight bend, squeeze your glutes and hamstrings to lift both legs upward through hip extension.
Pause briefly at the top of the movement while maintaining core tension, then slowly lower legs back to the starting position under control.
Repeat for desired repetitions, focusing on smooth controlled movement and avoiding momentum.
exercises.description: A supine hip-extension movement where you lie face up and lift the legs by contracting the glutes and hamstrings while keeping the torso stable; the abs and lower spine act to stabilize the pelvis throughout the movement.

exercises.name: Lying 90 degrees Alternate Leg Extension
exercises.notes: Start lying supine (on your back) with hips and knees both flexed to about 90 degrees (thighs vertical, shins horizontal).
instructions: Start lying supine (on your back) with hips and knees both flexed to about 90 degrees (thighs vertical, shins horizontal). Arms rest by your sides for support.
Brace your core and press your lower back lightly into the floor to prevent arching; keep hips level throughout the set.
Slowly extend one leg by straightening the knee until the leg is nearly straight (do not hyperextend or lock the knee). Maintain the hip at ~90 degrees (thigh stays vertical).
Pause briefly at the top with control, then inhale and slowly bend the knee to return that leg to the starting 90-degree position.
Immediately repeat the same extension with the opposite leg, alternating legs in a controlled rhythm.
Keep movement slow and deliberate, exhaling during the extension and inhaling during the return, and avoid letting the pelvis tilt or the lower back arch.
Perform desired reps or time, adjusting range of motion or tempo if you feel knee pain; stop if sharp pain occurs.
exercises.description: Lying supine with hips and knees bent at 90 degrees, extend one leg at a time by straightening the knee and then return to the start. This movement emphasizes knee extension to load the quadriceps while the core and glutes stabilize the pelvis.

exercises.name: Bicycle Crunch
exercises.notes: Lie supine on the floor with knees bent and feet lifted off the ground in a tabletop position (shins parallel to the floor).
instructions: Lie supine on the floor with knees bent and feet lifted off the ground in a tabletop position (shins parallel to the floor).
Place hands lightly behind your head or temples (do not interlock fingers behind the skull) and keep the chin slightly tucked.
Exhale and curl the torso up off the floor, rotating so one elbow moves toward the opposite knee while that knee is drawn in toward the chest.
Simultaneously extend the other leg straight out at about a 45-degree angle, keeping it off the floor and the knee soft.
Inhale as you return the torso toward the floor and switch sides, bringing the opposite elbow to the other knee while extending the first leg (pedaling motion).
Continue alternating in a controlled rhythm, avoiding pulling on the neck and maintaining engagement of the abs with the lower back pressed lightly into the floor.
Keep movement deliberate and driven by the core and rotation of the torso rather than momentum from the legs or hands.

instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and a slight natural arch in the low back.
Grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width, wrists stacked over elbows and thumbs wrapped around the bar.
Retract and depress the shoulder blades (pinch the shoulder blades together) to create a stable chest platform.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar over the chest so it is balanced and steady.
Inhale, lower the bar under control to the mid-chest (sternum) keeping elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso.
Pause briefly with the bar touching or just above the chest while maintaining tightness through the core and legs.
Exhale and drive the bar upward by extending the elbows, pressing the bar back to the start position while keeping wrists neutral.
Avoid excessive elbow flare or bouncing the bar off the chest; maintain a controlled tempo and keep feet planted.
After the set, carefully re-rack the bar by guiding it back onto the bench supports before releasing your grip.
exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a supine pressing exercise performed on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also working the triceps and anterior delts. The lifter lowers a barbell to the chest and presses it back to full arm extension under control.

exercises.name: Barbell Standing Shoulders Press
exercises.notes: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and knees slightly soft.
instructions: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and knees slightly soft.
Place the barbell in a front-rack position resting on the upper chest/clavicle area with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width and full grip around the bar.
Retract the shoulder blades slightly, keep the chest up and core braced (neutral spine); avoid excessive lumbar arching.
Take a full breath and begin the press by driving the bar upward in a straight line, slightly moving the head back as the bar passes and then returning the head forward under the bar.
Fully extend the elbows at the top with the bar directly over or slightly behind the head, shoulders stacked over hips and feet for balance.
Lower the bar under control back to the front-rack position to complete one rep while inhaling, keeping the elbows tracking slightly forward.
Repeat for desired repetitions, maintaining tight core engagement and controlled tempo; stop or reduce weight if you must excessively lean back to finish reps.
exercises.description: A standing barbell overhead press performed from the front-rack position that primarily develops the delts while requiring the triceps and upper back to assist and stabilize. The lifter presses the barbell from shoulder level to full arm extension overhead while maintaining a braced core.

exercises.name: Barbell Incline Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set an adjustable bench to a 30-45 degree incline, lie back with your head, upper back, and glutes firmly against the pad, and plant feet flat on the floor.
instructions: Set an adjustable bench to a 30-45 degree incline, lie back with your head, upper back, and glutes firmly against the pad, and plant feet flat on the floor.
Grip the barbell slightly wider than shoulder-width with palms facing away, unrack the bar and position it directly above your upper chest with arms extended.
Inhale and lower the bar with control to your upper chest near the collarbone, keeping elbows at a 45-60 degree angle from your torso.
Press the bar back up explosively while exhaling, extending your arms fully without locking the elbows aggressively.

exercises.name: Barbell Decline Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set a decline bench into a rack and place a barbell at a height you can safely unrack while lying down; secure your feet under the decline foot pads.
instructions: Set a decline bench into a rack and place a barbell at a height you can safely unrack while lying down; secure your feet under the decline foot pads.
Lie supine on the decline bench so your head is lower than your hips and your shoulders and back are flat against the bench; keep feet locked under the pads for stability.
Grasp the bar with a double overhand grip slightly wider than shoulder width (pronated grip); wrists stacked over elbows and shoulders pulled down and back.
Unrack the bar by straightening your arms and moving the bar out over your lower chest; maintain a controlled, tight torso and scapular retraction.
Inhale and slowly lower the bar in a controlled path to the lower chest/upper abdomen (sternal/xyphoid area) with elbows at roughly a 30–45° angle from the torso.
Pause briefly with the bar touching or just above the chest, then exhale and press the bar upward along the same path until arms are nearly extended.
Avoid flaring the elbows excessively and keep the feet anchored under the pads; use a spotter for heavy sets and re-rack the bar carefully after the set.
exercises.description: A decline barbell bench press performed lying supine on a decline bench with the head lower than the hips to emphasize the lower portion of the pectorals. The lifter lowers the bar to the lower chest/upper abdomen and presses it back up to full extension.

exercises.name: Barbell Rear Delt Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips and slightly bend the knees until your torso is roughly parallel to the floor (or at a 30–45° angle if mobility requires), keeping a neutral spine and chest up.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and a barbell on the floor in front of you.
Hinge at the hips and slightly bend the knees until your torso is roughly parallel to the floor (or at a 30–45° angle if mobility requires), keeping a neutral spine and chest up.
Grip the barbell with an overhand (pronated) grip slightly wider than shoulder width so elbows can travel out to the sides.
Brace your core and retract your shoulder blades, maintaining a stable torso throughout the movement.
Initiate the rep by driving the elbows outward and up, pulling the bar toward the upper chest/upper abdomen while keeping the elbows high to emphasize the rear delts.
At the top, pause briefly and squeeze the rear delts and upper back without shrugging the shoulders.
Slowly lower the bar under control back to the start position, fully extending the arms while maintaining the hip hinge and neutral spine.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions; avoid using momentum or excessive torso rotation to lift the weight.
exercises.description: A bent-over barbell row variation that specifically emphasizes the rear delts by rowing with elbows flared out to the sides. Performed from a hip-hinged standing position to target the posterior shoulder and upper back.

exercises.name: Barbell Rear Delt Raise
exercises.notes: Stand upright with feet hip-width apart and hold a barbell behind your body with an overhand grip, palms facing away from you.
instructions: Stand upright with feet hip-width apart and hold a barbell behind your body with an overhand grip, palms facing away from you.
Keep your chest up, core braced, and maintain an upright posture throughout the movement.
With arms relatively straight, lift the barbell backward and upward away from your body by squeezing the rear delts.
Raise the bar as high as comfortable while maintaining control, then lower slowly back to the starting position.
Avoid leaning forward or using momentum; focus on controlled movement through the rear deltoids.
exercises.description: A standing barbell exercise performed with the bar held behind the body using an overhand grip. The movement involves lifting the barbell backward and upward away from the glutes by extending the shoulders, keeping arms relatively straight to target the posterior deltoids and traps.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Barbell Bent Over Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is roughly 45 degrees to parallel to the floor; keep the spine neutral (no rounding) and engage the abs and glutes.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and the barbell over mid-foot; use an overhand (pronated) grip slightly wider than shoulder width.
Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is roughly 45 degrees to parallel to the floor; keep the spine neutral (no rounding) and engage the abs and glutes.
Hold the bar with arms extended and shoulders protracted, chest up and eyes looking a few feet in front of you to maintain a neutral neck.
Initiate the rep by driving the elbows back and up, pulling the bar toward your lower chest / upper abdomen while squeezing the shoulder blades together.
Keep the elbows close to the body (not flaring excessively) and avoid using momentum from the hips; breathe out as you pull.
Lower the bar slowly and under control to the starting position with arms extended while maintaining the hip hinge and neutral spine; breathe in on the descent.
Repeat for the desired reps, controlling tempo and keeping the torso stable — if you cannot maintain a flat back, reduce weight or stop and reset.
exercises.description: Standing hip-hinged barbell row where you pull a loaded barbell toward the lower chest/upper abdomen; primarily targets the lats and upper back while requiring posterior chain and core stabilization. Maintain a flat back and drive the elbows back to retract the shoulder blades.

exercises.name: Chin-ups (narrow parallel grip)
exercises.notes: Start by grasping parallel handles overhead with a narrow neutral grip (palms facing each other) and arms fully extended while hanging from the bar; cross your ankles or keep feet together and maintain a slight hollow body with ribs down.
instructions: Start by grasping parallel handles overhead with a narrow neutral grip (palms facing each other) and arms fully extended while hanging from the bar; cross your ankles or keep feet together and maintain a slight hollow body with ribs down.
Brace your core and retract your shoulder blades slightly to remove slack before initiating the pull (scapular set).
Exhale and pull your elbows down and back, driving the elbows toward your hips while focusing on pulling with the lats rather than just the arms.
Continue the pull until your chin clears or reaches the level of the handles and your chest approaches the grips; pause and squeeze the shoulder blades together at the top for 1 second.
Inhale and lower yourself slowly and under control back to a full hang with arms fully extended, maintaining shoulder tension and avoiding a sudden drop.
Keep the torso stable and avoid excessive swinging or kipping; if needed, use a controlled bend in the knees or a slight leg cross to prevent momentum.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, focusing on smooth tempo, full range of motion, and consistent breathing.
exercises.description: A narrow, neutral-grip chin-up performed from a vertical hang that emphasizes the latissimus dorsi while also engaging the arms and upper back. Pull the body up until the chin clears the handles, then lower under control to a full hang.

exercises.name: Barbell Paused Bent Over Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is roughly parallel to the floor (or at a comfortable angled bend); keep the back flat and head in neutral alignment.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and the barbell over mid-foot.
Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is roughly parallel to the floor (or at a comfortable angled bend); keep the back flat and head in neutral alignment.
Grip the bar with a shoulder-width overhand grip (or mixed grip if needed for heavy loads) and brace your core.
Initiate the movement by pulling your shoulder blades together and driving your elbows back, bringing the bar toward your lower chest/upper abdomen.
Hold and squeeze the shoulder blades together at the top for 1–2 seconds (the paused portion) while keeping the torso stable and avoiding excess torso rotation.
Lower the bar under control until arms are extended and the bar is near knee level or a natural hang, maintaining a neutral spine throughout.
Repeat for desired reps while breathing out during the pull and inhaling as you lower; avoid rounding the back or using excessive momentum.
exercises.description: A bent-over barbell row performed from a hip-hinged position with a brief pause at the top of the pull to maximize scapular retraction and time under tension. Emphasizes rowing the elbows back while keeping the torso stable and the spine neutral.

exercises.name: Barbell Pullover
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with your feet flat on the floor and your head and upper back supported; grip the barbell with an overhand grip about shoulder-width apart.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with your feet flat on the floor and your head and upper back supported; grip the barbell with an overhand grip about shoulder-width apart.
Start with the barbell held directly over your chest with your arms nearly extended and a slight bend in the elbows; brace your core and keep a neutral spine.
Slowly lower the barbell in a controlled arc backwards and over your head until you feel a stretch through the chest and lats (keep elbows slightly bent and fixed throughout the motion).
Pause briefly at the bottom of the pullover, then pull the bar back in the same arc to bring it over your chest by contracting your lats and pectorals.
From the chest position, press the barbell straight up overhead until arms are nearly extended to complete the press portion, then lower the bar back to the chest in control.
Repeat the pullover-to-press sequence for the desired reps; maintain core tension, avoid overarching the lower back, and use a spotter for heavier loads.
exercises.description: A supine barbell movement that combines a pullover (bar travels in an arc behind the head) with a press back to overhead, emphasizing a stretch and contraction of the chest while also recruiting the lats and triceps.

exercises.name: Barbell Bent Arm Pullover
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with your feet planted on the floor and your torso flat against the bench (supine position).
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with your feet planted on the floor and your torso flat against the bench (supine position).
Grasp a barbell with an overhand grip about shoulder-width apart and lift it straight up over your chest with elbows bent roughly 90 degrees; keep the elbows fixed throughout the movement.
Brace your core and pull your shoulder blades slightly down and back to stabilize the shoulders before beginning the descent.

Slowly lower the barbell in a controlled arc behind your head by allowing shoulder extension (keeping the elbow angle constant) until you feel a stretch in the lats/upper chest area or until the bar reaches a comfortable range of motion.

Pause briefly at the bottom while maintaining tension in the lats and serratus anterior, then pull the bar back over the chest by driving the shoulders into flexion using your lats and chest (exhale on the concentric).
Keep the movement smooth and controlled, avoiding excessive lower back arch; maintain the bent-elbow position and avoid turning the elbows outward or using momentum.
Perform the desired number of reps and then safely rack the barbell, or have a spotter assist when lowering the weight off your chest.
exercises.description: A supine barbell pullover performed with the elbows bent that moves the weight in an arc from over the chest to behind the head, stretching and loading the latissimus region while also recruiting the chest and triceps. Performed with a fixed elbow angle to emphasize shoulder extension and thoracic mobility.

exercises.name: Lever Alternating Pullover (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat height so the back pad supports your upper back and the lever handles sit just above/behind your head when seated upright.
instructions: Adjust the seat height so the back pad supports your upper back and the lever handles sit just above/behind your head when seated upright.
Load an appropriate plate weight and sit with your back flat against the pad, feet flat on the floor and knees bent; brace your core to maintain a neutral spine.
Grasp the right and left lever handles (one in each hand) with a neutral or overhand grip. Begin with both arms extended overhead with a slight bend in the elbows.

For each rep, exhale and pull one lever down and in a smooth arc toward your chest/upper stomach using the shoulder joint — lead with the upper arm, not the wrist, and keep the elbow slightly bent and fixed.
At the bottom of the movement, pause briefly and focus on squeezing the pectorals and lats on the working side while keeping your torso stationary.
Inhale and slowly return the lever under control back to the start (overhead) to complete the eccentric phase; avoid letting the shoulder snap back.
Alternate sides each repetition (right arm then left arm) while maintaining the same tempo, core bracing, and no excessive torso rotation or lumbar arch.
Keep movements controlled, avoid shrugging the shoulders, and reduce weight if you cannot maintain a fixed elbow angle and stable torso throughout the set.
exercises.description: Seated plate-loaded lever pullover is a single-arm/alternating machine pullover that moves the upper arm in an arc from overhead down toward the chest, emphasizing the pectorals while also recruiting the lats and serratus anterior. The movement is performed with a slight elbow bend and controlled eccentric return.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Lever Pullover (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Step 1: Sit on the machine with your back pressed firmly against the backrest, feet flat on the floor and knees bent at about 90 degrees. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Step 1: Sit on the machine with your back pressed firmly against the backrest, feet flat on the floor and knees bent at about 90 degrees.
Step 2: Adjust the seat and pivot so the lever handles start just above and behind your head when your arms are extended; select an appropriate weight.
Step 3: Reach up and grasp the handles with a comfortable (typically neutral or slightly pronated) grip, keeping a small, fixed bend in the elbows.
Step 4: Begin with the shoulders stretched overhead and the chest lifted slightly, maintaining a stable torso against the pad.
Step 5: Exhale and pull the lever in a controlled arc down and back toward your hips/upper thighs, leading with the elbows and focusing on squeezing the lats.
Step 6: Pause briefly at the bottom of the movement with elbows by your sides and a full lat contraction; avoid shrugging the shoulders.
Step 7: Inhale and slowly return the lever to the start position, resisting the weight and keeping tension on the lats without letting the shoulders fully relax.
Step 8: Repeat for desired reps; maintain strict form—no torso rocking, keep core engaged, and avoid locking the elbows at the end ranges.
exercises.description: Seated plate-loaded lever pullover that moves the arms in an overhead-to-hip arc to lengthen and contract the lats while also engaging the pectorals and serratus anterior. Performed on a lever machine with the torso supported by a backrest for stability.

exercises.description: Seated plate-loaded lever pullover is a single-arm/alternating machine pullover that moves the upper arm in an arc from overhead down toward the chest, emphasizing the pectorals while also recruiting the lats and serratus anterior. The movement is performed with a slight elbow bend and controlled eccentric return.

exercises.name: Lever Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Set the machine: choose an appropriate weight, adjust the seat height so the pivot of the lever lines up with your shoulder joint and the handles start near your hips. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Set the machine: choose an appropriate weight, adjust the seat height so the pivot of the lever lines up with your shoulder joint and the handles start near your hips.
Sit upright with your back pressed into the seat pad, feet flat on the floor, and chest up; grasp the handles with a neutral or slightly pronated grip.
Start with your elbows slightly bent and arms resting at your sides (not locked), maintaining a neutral wrist and steady grip.
Exhale and lift the handles out to your sides in a controlled arc, leading with the elbow so the upper arm reaches about parallel to the floor (shoulder height).
Keep your torso still and avoid shrugging your shoulders; use your delts to move the weight rather than momentum or back extension.
Pause briefly at the top with the elbows slightly higher than the wrists, feeling tension in the delts and traps for scapular stability.
Inhale and slowly lower the handles back to the start position with control, maintaining the slight elbow bend.
Repeat for the desired number of reps. Prioritize controlled tempo, full range without swinging, and maintain scapular stability throughout.
exercises.description: Seated lever lateral raise is a machine exercise that isolates the lateral head of the delts by abducting the arms out to the sides from a seated, supported position. It emphasizes controlled movement to raise the arms to about shoulder height while minimizing trunk movement.

exercises.name: Lever Lateral Raise with Chest Support
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat so your chest rests firmly against the pad and your feet are flat on the floor; the lever handles should be at about hip height when your arms hang down. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Adjust the seat so your chest rests firmly against the pad and your feet are flat on the floor; the lever handles should be at about hip height when your arms hang down.
Sit with your chest pressed into the pad, grip the handles with a neutral or overhand grip, and keep a small bend in the elbows throughout the movement.
Start with the arms hanging at your sides (shoulders relaxed) and brace your core to prevent movement of the torso.
Exhale and raise both arms out to the sides in a controlled arc until your upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor (about 90 degrees), keeping the elbows slightly bent and the wrists neutral.
Pause briefly at the top while avoiding shoulder shrugging (do not lift with the neck or upper trapezius).
Inhale and slowly lower the handles back to the start position under control, maintaining chest contact with the pad and minimizing momentum.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, choosing a weight that allows strict form without swinging.
exercises.description: Seated chest-supported lateral raise performed on a lever machine to isolate the lateral deltoids; the chest pad prevents torso swing so the delts do the work. Arms are raised out to the sides with a slight elbow bend and controlled tempo.

instructions: Stand with feet wider than shoulder-width apart, toes pointed outward at 30-45 degrees, with the barbell over your midfoot.
Hinge at the hips and bend knees to grip the bar with hands inside your legs, keeping chest up and back flat.

exercises.name: Barbell Clean and Press
exercises.notes: Drive through heels, explosively extend hips and knees while keeping the bar close to your body, shrugging forcefully at full extension.
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart, barbell over mid-foot, grip just outside knees with chest up and back flat.
Drive through heels, explosively extend hips and knees while keeping the bar close to your body, shrugging forcefully at full extension.
Pull elbows high and rotate them forward to catch the bar in a front rack position on your shoulders.
Stand tall to full hip and knee extension, then press the bar overhead until arms are fully locked out.
Lower the bar back to front rack, then return to the floor with control by hinging at hips and bending knees.
exercises.description: A compound Olympic-style lift that combines a barbell clean (explosive hip extension and pull to bring the bar from the floor to the front rack position) with an overhead press. The clean phase trains explosive power through the legs, hips, and posterior chain, while the press phase targets the shoulders. This full-body movement recruits multiple muscle groups for power generation and stability.

exercises.name: Barbell Standing Military Press
exercises.notes: Stand upright with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and the barbell resting on your front shoulders/clavicle in a front-rack position (hands just outside shoulder width, elbows slightly forward).
instructions: Stand upright with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and the barbell resting on your front shoulders/clavicle in a front-rack position (hands just outside shoulder width, elbows slightly forward).
Grip the bar with a firm overhand grip, wrists stacked under the bar and chest tall; take a full breath and brace your core and glutes to prevent lower-back arching.
Tighten your upper back and scapulae (retract slightly) and press your head back just enough to allow the bar to travel in a straight line.

Drive the barbell upward forcefully by extending the elbows and pressing through the shoulders until the arms are fully extended overhead; finish with the bar roughly over the midline of the body and the head moved slightly forward under the bar.
Pause briefly at the top with shoulders engaged and core braced, then inhale and lower the bar under control back to the starting front-rack position at the clavicle.
Perform repetitions with a steady tempo, keeping the torso rigid and avoiding excessive lumbar extension or forward collapse of the elbows.

If starting from the floor (no rack), use a controlled clean or push-press to bring the bar to the front-rack before beginning strict presses; lower weight to the floor only after finishing the set or rerack safely.
exercises.description: A standing barbell overhead press performed from the front-rack position that primarily develops the shoulder (delts) musculature while requiring upper-body pressing strength and core stability. Performed without a rack, the bar is pressed vertically from the shoulders to full arm extension overhead.

exercises.name: Barbell Shoulder Grip Upright Row
exercises.notes: Stand upright with feet hip-width apart, knees slightly bent and core braced (standing position).
instructions: Stand upright with feet hip-width apart, knees slightly bent and core braced (standing position).
Hold a barbell with a pronated (palms facing body) shoulder-width grip so the bar rests against your thighs with arms fully extended.
Keep your chest up, shoulders down and back, and maintain a neutral spine throughout.
Initiate the movement by driving your elbows upward and outward, leading with the elbows rather than the hands so the bar travels close to your body.

Lift the bar to about upper-chest / collarbone level or until your elbows are roughly shoulder height; avoid shrugging the shoulders excessively or lifting above shoulder level to reduce impingement risk.
Pause briefly at the top with control, then inhale and slowly lower the bar back to the starting position under control.
Breathe out on the concentric (lifting) phase and inhale on the eccentric (lowering) phase.
Use a controlled tempo and moderate weight to preserve form; stop the set if you feel shoulder pain and reduce range of motion or load as needed.
exercises.description: Standing barbell upright row performed with a shoulder-width (pronated) grip; the movement elevates the elbows and lifts the bar vertically toward the upper chest/collarbone, primarily working the delts and engaging traps and arm muscles.

exercises.name: Barbell Wide Grip Upright Row
exercises.notes: Stand upright with feet about hip-width apart and the barbell held in front of your thighs with a wide overhand grip (hands wider than shoulder width).
instructions: Stand upright with feet about hip-width apart and the barbell held in front of your thighs with a wide overhand grip (hands wider than shoulder width).
Brace your core, keep your chest up and a slight bend in the knees; let the bar hang at arm's length with wrists neutral.
Initiate the movement by driving your elbows up and out to the sides, pulling the bar straight up toward the upper chest/collarbone while keeping the bar close to the body.
Lead with the elbows so they finish higher than the hands; stop when the bar reaches about upper-chest level or when elbows are roughly shoulder height.
Pause briefly at the top, squeezing the delts and traps, then lower the bar under control back to the starting position.
Maintain an upright torso, avoid excessive torso lean or using momentum, and do not let the wrists excessively bend or the shoulders internally rotate to reduce risk of impingement.
exercises.description: A standing barbell movement performed with a wide overhand grip that elevates the elbows out and up to target the shoulder (deltoid) muscles with strong involvement of the traps. The wide grip emphasizes the lateral delts and reduces internal rotation at the shoulder.

exercises.name: Barbell Front Raise
exercises.notes: Stand upright with feet hip- to shoulder-width apart, knees soft and core braced (standing position).
instructions: Stand upright with feet hip- to shoulder-width apart, knees soft and core braced (standing position).
Hold the barbell with a double overhand (pronated) grip about shoulder-width apart, arms extended down with a slight bend in the elbows.
Start with the barbell resting against your thighs, chest up and shoulders back; keep a neutral spine.
Exhale and raise the barbell straight in front of you by flexing at the shoulder until the bar reaches about eye/shoulder level (arms roughly parallel to the floor).
Keep the elbows slightly bent and wrists neutral throughout; avoid swinging the torso or using momentum.
Pause briefly at the top with shoulders engaged, then inhale and slowly lower the barbell back to the starting position under control.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, maintaining a steady tempo and continuous core tension to prevent back arching.
exercises.description: Standing barbell front raise lifts a barbell from hip level to about shoulder height to isolate the anterior deltoids. The movement is performed with a pronated grip and controlled tempo to minimize momentum and maximize shoulder engagement.

exercises.name: Barbell Rear Delt Raise
exercises.notes: Stand upright with feet hip-width apart, holding a barbell behind your body with an overhand grip slightly wider than shoulder-width.
instructions: Stand upright with feet hip-width apart, holding a barbell behind your body with an overhand grip slightly wider than shoulder-width.
Keep your chest up, shoulders back, and maintain a slight bend in the elbows throughout the movement.
Exhale and lift the bar by driving your elbows out to the sides and slightly back, squeezing the rear delts at the top.
Raise until your upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor or slightly below shoulder height, pause briefly.
Inhale and slowly lower the bar back to the starting position under control, avoiding momentum.
exercises.description: Standing upright rear delt raise using a barbell held behind the body. The bar is lifted by driving the elbows out and back to target the posterior deltoids and upper back while maintaining an upright torso position.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.

Exhale and drive the bar upward by extending the elbows, pressing the bar back to the start position while keeping wrists neutral.
Avoid excessive elbow flare or bouncing the bar off the chest; maintain a controlled tempo and keep feet planted.
After the set, carefully re-rack the bar by guiding it back onto the bench supports before releasing your grip.

exercises.name: Barbell Decline Pullover
exercises.notes: Set a decline bench to a moderate angle and secure your feet under the pads so your body is stable and supine on the bench.
instructions: Set a decline bench to a moderate angle and secure your feet under the pads so your body is stable and supine on the bench.
Grasp a barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip about shoulder-width apart and lift it straight over your chest with elbows slightly bent.
Brace your core and keep your hips down to maintain a neutral spine throughout the movement.
Inhale and slowly lower the barbell in a controlled arc behind your head by hinging at the shoulders; keep a slight bend in the elbows and do not let them flare excessively.
Lower until you feel a deep stretch in the chest/lats or until the bar is roughly level with the crown of your head — do not go so far that your lower back overarches.
Exhale and pull the barbell back in the same arc to the start position over your chest by contracting the pectorals and lats; keep the movement smooth and controlled.
Repeat for the desired reps, maintaining steady tempo and full control on both lowering and lifting phases.
Use a spotter or lighter weight if you cannot control the bar safely behind your head; stop if you feel sharp shoulder or back pain.

exercises.name: Barbell Bent Arm Pullover
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with your feet planted on the floor and your torso flat against the bench (supine position).
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with your feet planted on the floor and your torso flat against the bench (supine position).
Grasp a barbell with an overhand grip about shoulder-width apart and lift it straight up over your chest with elbows bent roughly 90 degrees; keep the elbows fixed throughout the movement.
Brace your core and pull your shoulder blades slightly down and back to stabilize the shoulders before beginning the descent.

Slowly lower the barbell in a controlled arc behind your head by allowing shoulder extension (keeping the elbow angle constant) until you feel a stretch in the lats/upper chest area or until the bar reaches a comfortable range of motion.

Pause briefly at the bottom while maintaining tension in the lats and serratus anterior, then pull the bar back over the chest by driving the shoulders into flexion using your lats and chest (exhale on the concentric).
Keep the movement smooth and controlled, avoiding excessive lower back arch; maintain the bent-elbow position and avoid turning the elbows outward or using momentum.
Perform the desired number of reps and then safely rack the barbell, or have a spotter assist when lowering the weight off your chest.

Performed with a fixed elbow angle to emphasize shoulder extension and thoracic mobility.

exercises.name: Barbell Pendlay Row
exercises.notes: Grip the bar with a double overhand or mixed grip about shoulder-width apart, keep a neutral spine and eyes looking a few feet ahead on the floor.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart and the barbell over mid-foot; bend at the hips and knees to reach the bar so your torso is roughly parallel to the floor (hip hinge), knees slightly bent.
Grip the bar with a double overhand or mixed grip about shoulder-width apart, keep a neutral spine and eyes looking a few feet ahead on the floor.
Brace your core, pull your shoulder blades down and back, then explosively row the bar in a straight line to your lower chest/upper abdomen, driving your elbows back.

Lower the bar under control all the way back to the floor so it comes to a complete stop, reset any slack, then repeat for the desired reps.
Maintain a flat back and braced core throughout; if your lower back rounds or you must use momentum, reduce weight or raise the bar height.

workout.description: Chest/Back workout focusing on bodybuild and hit training.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.
instructions: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.

Lie supine on the bench with your head, upper back and glutes in contact with the bench, feet flat on the floor and a small natural arch in the lower back; retract and depress your shoulder blades to create a stable chest platform.
Grip the barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists should be stacked over the elbows and forearms vertical when the bar is over the chest.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar out over the chest while keeping the scapulae stable; lock the elbows briefly before starting the set.
Inhale and lower the bar in a controlled manner to the mid-chest/sternum (around nipple level), keeping the elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso to protect the shoulders.
Allow the bar to touch or come very close to the chest without bouncing; maintain tension in the pectorals and keep the feet and glutes braced.
Exhale and press the bar upward explosively while keeping the bar path slightly arced toward the hips until the arms are fully extended but not hyperextended.
Pause briefly at the top with the shoulders stable, then begin the next rep. Maintain consistent form and breathing throughout the set.
Use a spotter or safety pins in the rack for heavy sets, and avoid flaring the elbows excessively or letting the lower back lift off the bench.
exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a compound pressing movement performed lying supine on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also recruiting the triceps and anterior delts. It involves lowering a loaded barbell to the chest and pressing it back up to full arm extension with controlled technique.

exercises.name: Barbell Bent Over Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips, push the butt back, and bend the knees slightly so the torso is angled forward (near parallel to the floor), keeping a neutral spine and head in line with the spine.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart and the barbell over the midfoot; grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width using an overhand or mixed grip.
Hinge at the hips, push the butt back, and bend the knees slightly so the torso is angled forward (near parallel to the floor), keeping a neutral spine and head in line with the spine.
Brace your core and retract your shoulder blades slightly to set the upper back before the first rep.
Pull the barbell upward toward the lower chest/upper abdomen by driving the elbows back and keeping them close to the body; focus on squeezing the upper back at the top of the movement.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining a flat back, then lower the barbell under control until arms are nearly extended while preserving the hip-hinge position.
Breathe out while pulling the bar up and inhale while lowering it; keep the torso stable and avoid rounding the lower back or using excessive torso momentum.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, and reset your hip-hinge position between sets if you lose posture.
exercises.description: A hip-hinged barbell row that trains the upper back by pulling the loaded barbell toward the torso while maintaining a neutral spine. It also recruits the lats, arms and posterior chain for stability and power.

exercises.name: Barbell Pause Incline Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30-45 degrees and load the bar with appropriate weight; position safety pins or have a spotter if available.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30-45 degrees and load the bar with appropriate weight; position safety pins or have a spotter if available.
Lie supine on the incline bench with your head near the top, feet flat on the floor, knees bent and planted for stability.
Retract and squeeze your shoulder blades together to create a stable upper-back base; maintain a slight natural arch in your lower back.
Grip the bar with a medium (shoulder-width to slightly wider) grip, wrists stacked over elbows and elbows pointed roughly 45 degrees from your torso.
Unrack the bar and hold it straight above your shoulders with arms extended; take a full breath in and brace your core.
Lower the bar under control to the upper chest/clavicle area (not the throat), keeping the bar path slightly diagonal toward the upper chest.
Pause and hold the bar motionless on your chest for 1-2 seconds without bouncing; maintain tension in the pectorals and keep the scapulae retracted.
Exhale and press the bar upward explosively but under control until your arms are near full extension, avoiding locking out aggressively.
Repeat for the desired reps, then carefully rack the bar back on the uprights with control.
exercises.description: A barbell incline bench press with a deliberate pause at the bottom to increase time under tension and focus on the upper chest. The movement emphasizes controlled lowering to the upper chest/clavicular area, a short isometric pause, then a powerful press to near-full extension.

exercises.name: Lever Pullover (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Step 1: Sit on the machine with your back pressed firmly against the backrest, feet flat on the floor and knees bent at about 90 degrees.
instructions: Step 1: Sit on the machine with your back pressed firmly against the backrest, feet flat on the floor and knees bent at about 90 degrees.
Step 2: Adjust the seat and pivot so the lever handles start just above and behind your head when your arms are extended; select an appropriate weight.
Step 3: Reach up and grasp the handles with a comfortable (typically neutral or slightly pronated) grip, keeping a small, fixed bend in the elbows.
Step 4: Begin with the shoulders stretched overhead and the chest lifted slightly, maintaining a stable torso against the pad.
Step 5: Exhale and pull the lever in a controlled arc down and back toward your hips/upper thighs, leading with the elbows and focusing on squeezing the lats.
Step 6: Pause briefly at the bottom of the movement with elbows by your sides and a full lat contraction; avoid shrugging the shoulders.
Step 7: Inhale and slowly return the lever to the start position, resisting the weight and keeping tension on the lats without letting the shoulders fully relax.
Step 8: Repeat for desired reps; maintain strict form—no torso rocking, keep core engaged, and avoid locking the elbows at the end ranges.
exercises.description: Seated plate-loaded lever pullover that moves the arms in an overhead-to-hip arc to lengthen and contract the lats while also engaging the pectorals and serratus anterior. Performed on a lever machine with the torso supported by a backrest for stability.

exercises.name: Lever Alternating Pullover (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat height so the back pad supports your upper back and the lever handles sit just above/behind your head when seated upright.
instructions: Adjust the seat height so the back pad supports your upper back and the lever handles sit just above/behind your head when seated upright.
Load an appropriate plate weight and sit with your back flat against the pad, feet flat on the floor and knees bent; brace your core to maintain a neutral spine.
Grasp the right and left lever handles (one in each hand) with a neutral or overhand grip. Begin with both arms extended overhead with a slight bend in the elbows.

For each rep, exhale and pull one lever down and in a smooth arc toward your chest/upper stomach using the shoulder joint — lead with the upper arm, not the wrist, and keep the elbow slightly bent and fixed.
At the bottom of the movement, pause briefly and focus on squeezing the pectorals and lats on the working side while keeping your torso stationary.
Inhale and slowly return the lever under control back to the start (overhead) to complete the eccentric phase; avoid letting the shoulder snap back.
Alternate sides each repetition (right arm then left arm) while maintaining the same tempo, core bracing, and no excessive torso rotation or lumbar arch.
Keep movements controlled, avoid shrugging the shoulders, and reduce weight if you cannot maintain a fixed elbow angle and stable torso throughout the set.
exercises.description: Seated plate-loaded lever pullover is a single-arm/alternating machine pullover that moves the upper arm in an arc from overhead down toward the chest, emphasizing the pectorals while also recruiting the lats and serratus anterior. The movement is performed with a slight elbow bend and controlled eccentric return.

exercises.name: Barbell Decline Pullover
exercises.notes: Set a decline bench to a moderate angle and secure your feet under the pads so your body is stable and supine on the bench.
instructions: Set a decline bench to a moderate angle and secure your feet under the pads so your body is stable and supine on the bench.
Grasp a barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip about shoulder-width apart and lift it straight over your chest with elbows slightly bent.
Brace your core and keep your hips down to maintain a neutral spine throughout the movement.
Inhale and slowly lower the barbell in a controlled arc behind your head by hinging at the shoulders; keep a slight bend in the elbows and do not let them flare excessively.
Lower until you feel a deep stretch in the chest/lats or until the bar is roughly level with the crown of your head — do not go so far that your lower back overarches.
Exhale and pull the barbell back in the same arc to the start position over your chest by contracting the pectorals and lats; keep the movement smooth and controlled.
Repeat for the desired reps, maintaining steady tempo and full control on both lowering and lifting phases.
Use a spotter or lighter weight if you cannot control the bar safely behind your head; stop if you feel sharp shoulder or back pain.
exercises.description: Lying supine on a decline bench, the barbell pullover moves the weight in an arc from over the chest to behind the head, emphasizing a deep stretch and contraction of the chest while also engaging the lats and triceps.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Every working set should reach true muscular failure.

exercises.name: Lever Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Set the machine: choose an appropriate weight, adjust the seat height so the pivot of the lever lines up with your shoulder joint and the handles start near your hips.
instructions: Set the machine: choose an appropriate weight, adjust the seat height so the pivot of the lever lines up with your shoulder joint and the handles start near your hips.
Sit upright with your back pressed into the seat pad, feet flat on the floor, and chest up; grasp the handles with a neutral or slightly pronated grip.
Start with your elbows slightly bent and arms resting at your sides (not locked), maintaining a neutral wrist and steady grip.
Exhale and lift the handles out to your sides in a controlled arc, leading with the elbow so the upper arm reaches about parallel to the floor (shoulder height).
Keep your torso still and avoid shrugging your shoulders; use your delts to move the weight rather than momentum or back extension.
Pause briefly at the top with the elbows slightly higher than the wrists, feeling tension in the delts and traps for scapular stability.
Inhale and slowly lower the handles back to the start position with control, maintaining the slight elbow bend.
Repeat for the desired number of reps. Prioritize controlled tempo, full range without swinging, and maintain scapular stability throughout.
exercises.description: Seated lever lateral raise is a machine exercise that isolates the lateral head of the delts by abducting the arms out to the sides from a seated, supported position. It emphasizes controlled movement to raise the arms to about shoulder height while minimizing trunk movement.

exercises.name: Lever Assisted Triceps Pushdown
exercises.notes: Stand facing the machine with feet hip-width apart and grasp the lever handles with a neutral or overhand grip at chest level.
instructions: Stand facing the machine with feet hip-width apart and grasp the lever handles with a neutral or overhand grip at chest level.
Position your upper arms close to your sides with elbows fixed in place and maintain an upright torso with core braced.
Press the handles downward by extending only at the elbows until arms are fully straight, keeping shoulders down and back.
Pause briefly at full extension to squeeze the triceps, then slowly return to the starting position with controlled resistance.
Maintain constant tension throughout the movement and avoid using momentum or torso lean to move the weight.
exercises.description: Standing lever-assisted triceps pushdown where the elbows remain tucked and the forearms push the lever downward/backward to extend the elbow and isolate the triceps. The exercise uses a machine lever handle for resistance and stability.

exercises.name: Lever Preacher Curl
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat height so your chest is close to the preacher pad and your upper arms (from armpit to elbow) rest flat on the angled pad; sit with feet flat on the floor.
instructions: Adjust the seat height so your chest is close to the preacher pad and your upper arms (from armpit to elbow) rest flat on the angled pad; sit with feet flat on the floor.
Select the desired weight on the lever machine and grasp the handles with a supinated (palms-up) grip, wrists neutral.
Start with your arms extended (a small, comfortable bend in the elbow to avoid full lockout) and shoulders relaxed against the pad.
Exhale and curl the lever by flexing only at the elbows, bringing the handles toward your face while keeping your upper arms pressed to the pad.
Pause and squeeze the biceps at the top of the movement for 1–2 seconds without lifting your torso or using momentum.
Inhale and slowly lower the lever back to the start in a controlled manner until your forearms are near full extension against the pad.
Keep the movement strict: do not rock your torso, lift your elbows off the pad, or use shoulder movement; maintain steady tempo and full control throughout each repetition.
exercises.description: Seated lever preacher curl performed on a preacher/lever machine to isolate the biceps by stabilizing the upper arm on an angled pad and using elbow flexion to lift the lever. Emphasizes strict movement with minimal shoulder involvement.

exercises.name: Lever Hammer Grip Preacher Curl
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat so your upper arms rest flat and comfortably on the preacher pad with elbows just over the pad edge; select an appropriate weight on the lever machine.
instructions: Adjust the seat so your upper arms rest flat and comfortably on the preacher pad with elbows just over the pad edge; select an appropriate weight on the lever machine.
Sit upright with chest against the pad, feet flat on the floor, and maintain a neutral (thumbs-up) hammer grip on the handles.
Start with your arms extended toward the lever’s low position while keeping a slight bend in the elbows (do not lock joints).
Exhale and curl the lever upward by flexing at the elbow, keeping your upper arms pressed to the pad and moving only the forearms.
Pause briefly at the top when you feel a full biceps contraction (handles near shoulder level), avoiding shoulder shrugging or body swing.
Inhale and slowly lower the lever back to the start position under control until your arms are nearly extended, maintaining tension on the muscles.
Repeat for the prescribed reps, focusing on smooth tempo and strict form; if you feel torso movement, reduce weight.
exercises.description: Seated lever preacher curl using a neutral (hammer) grip to isolate the biceps while emphasizing the brachioradialis/forearm muscles; performed on a preacher pad to minimize shoulder involvement and momentum.

exercises.name: Lever Pronated Grip Seated Scapula Retraction Shrug (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat height and load the machine so you can reach the handles with your arms extended and feet flat on the footrests; sit upright with a neutral spine and knees bent.
instructions: Adjust the seat height and load the machine so you can reach the handles with your arms extended and feet flat on the footrests; sit upright with a neutral spine and knees bent.
Grasp the handles with a pronated (overhand) grip and let your shoulders relax down and slightly forward to set the start position.
Initiate the movement by pulling the handles back using your shoulder blades: actively retract (pinch) the scapulae together while keeping the torso stable and chest neutral.
As you retract the scapulae, elevate your shoulders into a controlled shrug (shoulder elevation) and squeeze your traps and upper back at the top for 1–2 seconds.
Slowly reverse the motion: lower the shoulders and allow the scapulae to protract back to the start position under control — do not let the weight slam forward.
Keep the arms relatively straight with a slight bend at the elbows, avoid using momentum or torso swing, and breathe out during the contraction and in during the return.
exercises.description: Seated, pronated-grip plate-loaded exercise that emphasizes scapular retraction and an upward shrug to target the trapezius and upper back. Performed from a seated position using the machine handles to actively pinch the shoulder blades together and elevate the shoulders.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Every working set should reach true muscular failure.
